coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose polymer dots and multi-purpose applications in pH and glucose sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Jiamin
Wang, Zhongrui
Zhao, Yanan
Gao, Yue
Xing, Huan
Song, Yuxin
Yang, Guang
Hou, Juan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose polymer dots (B-PDs) were synthesized by a straightforward route under mild conditions using ethylenediamine-modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic acid as precursors. The B-PDs exhibit a uniform nanoscale size distribution, good water solubility, photostability and excellent fluorescence emission with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 21.6 %. The B-PDs display a reversible fluorescence response with blue emission in acidic solution and green emission in alkaline solution. Moreover, the B-PDs can be designed as an excellent fluorescent sensor for glucose detection. After the addition of glucose, the FL intensity at λex = 388 nm can be enhanced due to the assembly of glucose on the B-PDs based on the reversible reaction between boronic acid and cis-diols through the aggregation-induced enhancement effect (AIE). Two good linear relationships can be obtained between (F-F0)/F0 and the glucose amount ranging from 0 ∼ 0.005 M (R2 = 0.9971) and 0.01 ∼ 1.2 M (R2 = 0.9984), and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 52 μM (3σ/slope). Meanwhile, the proposed probe was successfully utilized to analyze glucose in human urine samples (recovery: 97.9–104.1 %, RSD: 2.64–4.23 %) by the standard addition method, indicating good feasibility as a practical platform for sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Fluorescent sensor
Glucose detection
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of boronic acid-modified polymer dots (B-PDs)
Glucose detection based on the B-PDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image and particle size distribution of B-PDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of B-PDs. (b) Entire XPS spectrum, and high-resolution spectra of C1s (c), N1s (d), O1s (e) and B1s (f) of the B-PDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of B-PDs (inset photos of the B-PDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm light irradiation (right), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of B-PDs with different excitation wavelength.

(a) The photographs of the B-PDs under natural light (upper) and 365 nm UV irradiation (lower) in an aqueous solution from different values (from 2 to 14), (b) pH effect on the fluorescence maximum emission intensity at 501 nm and (c) fluorescence spectra of the B-PDs different pH value solutions, λexi: 388 nm.

(a) The fluorescence response of the B-PDs with the addition of glucose from 0 ∼ 1.5 M, (b) FL intensity change at 501 nm with glucose concentration (0 ∼ 1.5 M). Linear relationship between the (F-F0)/F0 and the glucose concentration ranging from (c) 0.0001 ∼ 0.01 M, and (d) 0.01 ∼ 1.2 M, λexi: 388 nm.
Schematic showing the preparation and applications of B-PDs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose polymer dots and multi-purpose applications in pH and glucose sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photofunctional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study describes a facile “synthesis-modification integration” strategy for the preparation of boronic acid modified polymer dots (B-PDs) based on the self-assembly of ethylenediamine-modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic under mild conditions.. The B-PDs exhibited reversible fluorescence response in acid solutions (blue emission) and alkaline solutions (green emission). Additionally, glucose could assemble on the surface of B-PDs due to the reaction between boronic acid and cis-diols, resulting in the FL intensity increase based on the aggregation induced enhancement effect. Additionally, good results could be obtained in real human urine samples analysis.

Herein, fluorescent carboxymethyl cellulose polymer dots (B-PDs) were synthesized by a straightforward route under mild conditions using ethylenediamine-modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic acid as precursors. The B-PDs exhibit a uniform nanoscale size distribution, good water solubility, photostability and excellent fluorescence emission with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 21.6 %. The B-PDs display a reversible fluorescence response with blue emission in acidic solution and green emission in alkaline solution. Moreover, the B-PDs can be designed as an excellent fluorescent sensor for glucose detection. After the addition of glucose, the FL intensity at λex = 388 nm can be enhanced due to the assembly of glucose on the B-PDs based on the reversible reaction between boronic acid and cis-diols through the aggregation-induced enhancement effect (AIE). Two good linear relationships can be obtained between (F-F0)/F0 and the glucose amount ranging from 0 ∼ 0.005 M (R2 = 0.9971) and 0.01 ∼ 1.2 M (R2 = 0.9984), and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 52 μM (3σ/slope). Meanwhile, the proposed probe was successfully utilized to analyze glucose in human urine samples (recovery: 97.9–104.1 %, RSD: 2.64–4.23 %) by the standard addition method, indicating good feasibility as a practical platform for sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The level of living for people has greatly increased due to technological and economic advancements, which has already had an impact on lifestyle and food habits. Owing to the intake of more high-calorie foods and lack of exercise, numerous diseases have emerged and pose a threat to human health. As a representative kind of chronic disease, diabetes mellitus can cause many different tissues, such as blood vessels, kidneys, heart and eyes [1–3]. According to the statistics from the World Health Organization, over 570 million people worldwide will suffer from diabetes mellitus in 2030. To date, the only way to diagnose diabetes mellitus is by measuring blood glucose levels. Diabetes mellitus can be diagnosed when blood glucose levels remain above 11.1 mM for at least two hours after meals [4,5]. To delay the occurrence and progression of diabetes-related complications, patients require strict blood glucose monitoring by injecting insulin to adjust the concentration of blood glucose [6]. However, blood glucose monitoring requires a higher frequency, expensive blood glucose meter and test paper, and interferences in the blood matrix may influence the reliability of the blood glucose concentration. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a daily blood glucose monitoring method with the advantages of low cost, fast, reliable sensitivity and convenience.

Recently, various methodologies for glucose detection have been developed, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), electrochemical-based methods, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), hexokinase method, fluorescent and optical techniques, etc. [7–22]. Among these methods, fluorescent detection method has attracted extensive attention because of its distinct benefits of simple operation, sensitivity, affordability and visual discrimination. To date, a number of fluorescent sensors, including quantum dots [2,4], metal–organic frameworks [13,14], carbon dots [17,18], fluorescent dyes [23], upconversion nanoparticles [24] and fluorescent polymers [25], have been established based on fluorescence quenching strategy, which are typically easy to operate and sensitive. However, some fluorescent sensors require complicated synthetic routes and are usually influenced by environmental conditions, which restrict their potential applications.

Fluorescent nonconjugated polymer dots (PDs) have drawn increasing attention as promising alternative fluorescent probes because of their simple preparation, rich functional groups, good water dispersibility, and excellent optical properties [26–31]. PDs are often prepared from polymer precursors through dehydration, condensation, entanglement and cross-linking, which exhibit the dual properties of carbon materials and aggregated polymers. However, the precursors for PDs preparation are mainly based on poly (ether amide), polydopamine or polyethyleneimine. Therefore, it is of great significance to design PD-based sensors, especially those based on naturally renewable polymers. Recently, our group designed novel PDs by using carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) as the precursor [32,33], which provides a new strategy for the fluorescent detection of glucose.

Herein, an effective and facile strategy to prepare boronic acid-modified polymer dots (B-PDs) was proposed by using ethylenediamine-modified carboxymethylcellulose (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic acid as precursors through a simple one-step method under mild conditions (Scheme 1 ). Compared to other methods, our proposed method can prevent the boric acid groups on the surface of the B-PDs from mutating and aggregating. The as-prepared B-PDs can be used for accurate quantitative analysis of pH values and glucose concentrations. Moreover, preliminary research demonstrates that B-PDs are anticipated to be a practical sensing platform for human urine glucose detection.

Phenylboronic acid and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, viscosity: 600–3000 MPa.s) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. in Shanghai, China. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. in Shanghai. Inorganic salts and other amino acids were obtained from Kermel Chemical Reagents Development Centre (Tianjin, China). All the solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

FT-IR spectra were captured using a 400 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used to record the fluorescence (FL) spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2550 PC spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). An FEI TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope from Netherlands was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. By using a monochromic X-ray source (Al Kaline, 1486.6 eV) and an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained. An XRD-6100 (Shimadzu) was used to capture the X-ray diffraction (XRD) data, and a laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (DXR2) was used to obtain the Raman spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of boronic acid-modified polymer dots (B-PDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The B-PDs were prepared by using carboxymethylcellulose derivative (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic acid as precursors using a simple one-step method with magnetic stirring under mild conditions, and CMC-NH2 was synthesized according to our previously reported works [32,33]. Briefly, a round-bottom flask was filled with 5 mL of CMC-NH2 (0.5 mg mL−1) aqueous solution, and 200 mg of phenylboronic acid was added to the aqueous solution. After ultrasound treatment for 30 min, the reaction mixture stirred for 24 h at 60 °C. The resultant B-PD solution was purified after being centrifuged at 1200 rpm for 5 min. Then, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed for three days using a 1000 MW dialysis bag. The pale-yellow B-PD powders were dried in a freeze-drying oven for 24 h.

Quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.546, refractive index n = 1.33, QY = 54.6 %) was used as reference material and dissolved in H2SO4 at a concentration of 0.1 mol/L, and the quantum yield (QY) of the B-PDs was calculated as follows:
QY =
Q R
I /
I R
O
D R
/ O D
n 2
/
n R 2
QY represents the fluorescence quantum yield for the B-PDs, I stands for the integrated emission intensity of the B-PDs as measured, OD stands for the optical density (typically less than 0.05) detected by UV–vis spectroscopy, and n represents the refractive index. The standard sample is indicated by subscript R.

The filter with rectangle shape was soaked into B-PDs solutions for 40 s, and air-dried for 5 min. Solutions with different pH values were injected into a pen for writing patterns on the test paper, and recorded pictures under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

Different amounts of glucose solutions were added to B-PD aqueous solutions (pH = 6) and reacted for 30 min, and then recorded the FL spectra with λexi = 388 nm. The maximum emission intensities at 501 nm were used for the quantitative analysis.

Human urine was selected as the real sample to evaluate the glucose detection ability of the B-PDs, and the samples were provided by diabetic patient volunteers, all of whom agreed to be used for experimental research. Fresh human urine samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter. Different amounts of glucose solutions and B-PDs were added to the urine sample and reacted for 30 min under gentle shaking. Then, the FL spectra were record with λexi = 388 nm at a slit width of 5 nm. Meanwhile, a commercial glucose assay kit was selected as the standard method using a local hospital-used instrument (SysmexUC3500) for the comparison with the as-prepared method.

Inspired by the fact that polymer precursors with amino groups can be used to prepare polymer dots, B-PDs were synthesized by using a carboxymethylcellulose derivative (CMC-NH2) and phenylboronic acid as precursors under mild conditions. The synthetic route for the B-PDs was shown in Scheme 1. The obtained B-PDs display excellent water solubility and a transparent pale-yellow color. TEM characterization was carried out to characterize the morphology and particle size of the B-PDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the as-prepared B-PDs exhibit no obvious aggregation and demonstrate a spherical structure with an average diameter of 2.2 ± 1.1 nm. It is speculated that primary and secondary amines of CMC-NH2 can not only react with phenylboronic acid, thus forming aromatic nucleus and C = N bonds, but also assist in the single-pot synthesis of N-doped PDs. Then, the above structures began to fold, shrink and self-assemble into nanoparticles in water, and formed the B-PDs [34]. The XRD pattern in Fig. S1 demonstrates a wide peak at 2θ = 21.6°, indicating a the disordered graphitic structure [35]. The Raman spectrum shows two typical peaks located at 1352 and 1602 cm−1 in Fig. S2, which correspond to the D and G bands of carbon materials, respectively. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.92, revealing the amorphous carbon phase of the B-PDs.

FT-IR and XPS spectra were used to investigate the surface composition and structure of the B-PDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, a broad absorption peak located at 3426 cm−1 represents the overlapping of O-H and N-H bonds. The band located at 1644 cm−1 stands for the bending vibrations of the N-H band of –CO-NH–, and the band at 1256 cm−1 refers to the stretching vibration of C-N bands, thus indicating that dehydration and condensation reactions occurred. Peaks at 1335 and 1070 cm−1 represent the B-O stretching vibration and C-B stretching vibration, respectively [4]. The above results demonstrated that the boronic acid groups were successfully linked on the B-PDs. Meanwhile, the chemical compositions of the B-PDs were characterized by XPS analysis. It can be seen that four elements, C, N, O, and B, exist in the B-PDs, and the typical binding energy peaks for C1s, N1s, O1s and B1s are located at 284.9 eV, 400.6 eV, 530.4 eV and 190.1 eV with the percentages of 65.87 %, 6.15 %, 20.20 % and 7.78 %, respectively (Fig. 2b). Three main types of C1s peaks exist in the high-resolution spectrum at 283.6, 285.5, and 287.7 eV, standing for C = C, C-N, and C = N/C = O groups, respectively (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution of N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d reveals three peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.6 eV, which stand for C-N, C-NH-C, and C = N-N, respectively. The O1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 530.1, 530.8 and 531.7 eV, which are attributed to O = C-N, O = C-OH and C-OH, respectively (Fig. 2e). In addition, the B1s spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 190.1 and 190.7 eV, referring to the C-B and B-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2f). The XPS results are consistent with the FT-IR data, indicating that the phenylboronic acid groups were successfully modified on the surface of the B-PDs.

The optical properties of the B-PDs were studied by UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectral analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, broad absorption peak centered at 340 nm and 415 nm is assigned to the electron transitions of n-π* for C═O and n-π for C═N bonds, respectively [29]. Meanwhile, the pale-yellow B-PDs show a bright blue-green color emission under the irradiation with 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a), and the maximum excitation (λexi) and emission (λem) peaks are 388 nm and 501 nm, respectively. By using quinine sulfate as the reference, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 21.6 %, which was slightly higher than that of other reported polymer dots and is suitable for sensing applications [26–33], which was ascribed to the doping of N atoms that increased the quantum yield of B-PDs. Moreover, when the excitation wavelength is changed from 350 to 460 nm, the B-PDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior, and the emission peak shows an obvious redshift (Fig. 3b), which is ascribed to the irregular surface defects and quantum confinement effects [36].

Additionally, the effect of pH on the B-PDs was also examined. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the B-PDs display interesting pH sensitive properties. The B-PDs display stronger fluorescence emission in neutral solutions, but the FL intensity at 501 nm decreases when the B-PDs are disseminated in strongly acidic or alkaline solutions (Fig. 4b). As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence emission displays an obvious a redshift (nearly 50 nm) as the pH increases from 1 to 14, and a bright blue or green color for the B-PDs solution can be observed in the acidic or in alkaline solutions, respectively. Hence, the fluorescence emission color of the B-PDs solution can be directly adjusted by changing the pH value. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectra in Fig. S3 indicated that the n-π for C═N bonds transition shifted from 415 nm to 470 nm when the pH value was in alkaline solutions. As shown in Fig. S4, the FT-IR spectra of the B-PDs at different pH values showed that the typical peaks at 1644 cm−1 for the bending vibrations of N-H decreased, thus indicating the deprotonation process in alkaline solutions. Therefore, similar to the reported works, owing to the presence of ionizable surface functional groups on the surface of B-PDs, they are vulnerable to pH changes with protonation-deprotonation processes, thus changing the surface states of B-PDs and resulting in a change in fluorescence emission [29,37]. Based on the excellent pH sensitivity of the B-PDs, fluorescent test paper was prepared for visual sensing of pH. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the FL can be quenched by acidic solutions and the quenched images can be observed by the naked eye. The above results indicated that the test paper containing the B-PDs could be used for direct acid recognition. Interestingly, two good linear relationships can be observed between the FL intensity at 501 nm and pH values ranging from 1 to 4 and 10–14, thus indicating that the pH value can be quantitatively analyzed based on the B-PDs fluorescent method (Fig. S6). In addition, the photostability of the B-PDs was studied by measuring the FL intensity at 501 nm with extending the irradiation time of 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. S7, the FL intensity at 501 nm remained almost stable after 48 h of exposure, implying the outstanding photostability of the B-PDs. In addition, the concentration of NaCl also has little effect on the fluorescence emission, thus endowing them with great potential for fluorescence imaging and sensing (Fig. S8). Moreover, the fluorescence emission intensity did not change much during 6 months of long-term storage at 4 °C, indicating excellent time-stability (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose detection based on the B-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Previous work reported that boronic acids can react with cis-diols to form stable boronate complexes with reversibility, the carboxymethylcellulose based B-PDs modified with phenylboronic acid units were speculated to be utilized for the detection of glucose. To obtain stable experimental data, the optimum pH value was 6, and the incubation time was 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the B-PDs solution displays strong FL emission at 501 nm upon excitation at 388 nm, and the fluorescence spectra show an obvious enhancement and a blueshift with the addition of glucose. Meanwhile, the lifetimes of the B-PDs, B-PDs-5 mM glucose, B-PDs-500 mM, and B-PDs-1000 mM glucose were calculated to be 3.2, 3.2, 3.2 and 3.3 ns, respectively (Fig. S10). The excited state lifetime displays a static nature process without any obvious discernible change with the addition of glucose. As shown in Fig. S11, the typical peak located at 1355 cm−1 stands for the C-O stretching vibration of boronate ester, which demonstrated the formation of boronate ester complex between the cis-diols of glucose and boronic acids [38,39]. It is speculated that the boronate ester complex can lead to aggregation of the B-PDs and change the surface recombination annihilation (Fig. S12). The high energy barrier for intramolecular motions blocks the non-radiative relaxation mechanism and favors the radiative decay, which leads to fluorescence enhancement of the B-PDs via the aggregation-induced enhancement effect [29,40]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the FL intensity at 501 nm increased with increasing glucose concentration (0.0001 ∼ 1.2 M). Moreover, the fluorescence changes can be summarized by the following Stern-Volmer equation:
(F − F0)/F0 = 0.0011 + 11.53 Cglucose, (Cglucose = 0 ∼ 0.005 M)

Two good linear relationships can be obtained between (F-F0)/F0 and the glucose amount ranging from 0 ∼ 0.005 M (R2 = 0.9971) and 0.01 ∼ 1.2 M (R2 = 0.9984) (Fig. 5c and 5d). Based on 3σ/k, where is the standard deviation of the blank signals with 11 parallel measurements and k is the slope of the calibration plot, the detection limit (LOD) for glucose was determined to be 52 μM. In comparison with other works on glucose detection, this work presents an easy approach for the synthesis of fluorescent B-PDs with a low LOD and a wider linear range for glucose detection (Table S1).

The as-prepared B-PDs display excellent reproducibility with a relative standard deviation of 1.77 % (Fig. S13). In addition, the selectivity for glucose detection was also studied. In the presence of 0.6 M of other metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cr3+), anion ions (F−, Br−,Cl−, I−, SO4 2−,NO3 −, CO3 2−, PO4 3−) and small organic molecules (glutamic acid, proline, tryptophan, arginine, tyrosine, lysine and aspartic acid), the FL intensity of the B-PDs exhibits a slight negligible change except for Ag+, which indicates that the B-PD-based fluorescent sensor can not only show outstanding selectivity toward glucose detection, but can also be utilized for detecting Ag+ (Fig. S14, S15 and S16).

It is of great significance for exploring the practicability and reliability of B-PDs-based sensing platform. The as-prepared B-PDs sensor was used to analyze the glucose concentration in diabetic patient urine samples through the conventional addition method. As shown in Table S2, the RSD values changed from 2.64 % to 4.23 % and the recoveries ranged from 97.9 % to 104.1 % for glucose detection with satisfactory inter/intra-day precisions (Table S3) for glucose detection The values obtained by this method did not differ significantly from those obtained via conventional enzymatic methods. All the above findings are satisfactory and show the possibility for practical applications of glucose detection in actual samples.

In summary, fluorescent phenylboronic acid-functionalized polymer dots (B-PDs) were synthesized by focusing on phenylboronic acid and carboxymethylcellulose through a synthesis-modification integration strategy. The B-PDs display a uniform size distribution and strong blue-green fluorescence emission with a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 21.6 %. The fluorescence emission color of the B-PDs solution can be directly adjusted by changing the pH value, which indicating that the pH value can be quantitatively analyzed based on the B-PDs fluorescent method. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emissions of the B-PDs can be enhanced by the addition of glucose. A sensitive fluorescent sensor for detecting glucose was established with the advantages of preparation facility, high sensitivity, wide detection range and low LOD. Moreover, the B-PDs-based sensing platform can also be used to predict the concentration of glucose in human urine samples, indicating great practical applications for glucose detection with facile and sensitive operation.
The datasets used or analyzed in this work are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
Not applicable.
The article does not contain any experiments with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Consent to participate was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Consent for publication was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Jiamin Liang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhongrui Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision. Yanan Zhao: Data curation, Supervision. Yue Gao: Writing – review & editing. Huan Xing: Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yuxin Song: Writing – review & editing. Guang Yang: . Juan Hou: Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109761.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
